title: Charcoal kaolin face mask
subtitle: A dry-mix clay face mask with kaolin clay and activated charcoal
excerpt: A dry-mix face mask of white kaolin clay and a small amount of activated charcoal for oily or congested skin. Stored as a powder; mix one teaspoon with one teaspoon of water in the palm just before use. Sits on the face for 10 minutes and rinses cleanly.
sourceNotes: Standard clay mask formulating ratios per cosmetic chemist guides: kaolin 85-95%, activated charcoal 5-15%, fine herb powder 0-5%. Dry mix is shelf-stable indefinitely; wet mask is mixed single-use to avoid preservation challenges.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 x 100 ml jar (~50 g dry mix; about 30 single-use masks at 1.5 g per use)
recipe.batchNotes: Triples cleanly for a 150 g jar that lasts 3-4 months at twice-weekly use.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dry mix is shelf-stable for 2 years. Single-use mixing avoids any preservation question on the wet mask.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 2 years sealed in a dry jar. Clay and charcoal are both stable indefinitely; the only failure mode is dampness, which clumps the mix and (over time) lets mould grow. Keep the jar sealed and dry; use a dry spoon.

glossaryTerms.definition: A clay mask works by drying on the skin. As the water evaporates, the clay tightens against the skin and pulls oil, sweat, and surface debris into the porous clay structure. White kaolin is the gentlest cosmetic clay (low cation-exchange capacity); bentonite and French green clay are stronger. Charcoal added at 5-15% boosts adsorption without changing the wet handling.

A wet cosmetic product (a clay mask mixed with water and stored as a paste) needs a broad-spectrum preservative or it spoils within days. A dry powder mix mixed single-use sidesteps preservation entirely - the powder is shelf-stable for years and only the immediate dose is hydrated. Many traditional cosmetic recipes follow this pattern.

content.text: and the clay pulls surface oil and debris into its porous structure as the water evaporates. Kaolin is the gentlest cosmetic clay - suitable for normal and sensitive skin; bentonite is stronger and suits oily; French green clay is stronger still. This is a kaolin base with a small charcoal addition for a slightly stronger pull without the tightening sting of a bentonite mask.
and mixed single-use, the recipe sidesteps preservation. The jar lasts 2 years. Each use takes about 30 seconds to mix in the palm.

items.prepNote: White kaolin clay, cosmetic grade. Sifted through a fine sieve if it clumps in the bag.
Food-grade activated charcoal. About 10% of the dry mix. The mix turns mid-grey when blended.

content.text: Sift the kaolin clay through a fine sieve into the small mixing bowl - the sieve catches the inevitable small lumps. Add the activated charcoal. Stir thoroughly with a dry spoon until the mix is a uniform mid-grey, about 1 minute. Spoon into a clean dry 100 ml jam jar. Lid and label with the date. Keep dry; do not store on the bathroom shelf in the steam zone.

Wash and dry the face. Scoop 1 level teaspoon of dry mix into a clean palm or small bowl. Add 1 teaspoon of cool water. Stir with a clean fingertip to a smooth paste. Apply with fingers across the forehead, nose, chin, and any other oily areas, avoiding the under-eye skin and mouth. Leave 8-10 minutes; the mask will lighten in colour and feel tight. Rinse off with warm water and pat the face dry. Follow with a light moisturiser if your skin feels tight. Once or twice a week is plenty.
Sealed jar in a dry cupboard. 2 years. Use a dry spoon every time; even small amounts of water in the jar can clump the mix. Discard if the powder feels damp or smells musty.

Patch test on the inner forearm for 24 hours before the first face use. Skip on broken skin, active eczema or rosacea flares, or freshly shaved skin. Avoid the eye area. Do not allow the mask to dry to cracking-tight; that point is over-dry and starts pulling moisture from the skin rather than oil from the surface.
